claim_reviews.claim: The semigroup Γ_{1,...,5} acts transitively on (Z/pZ)^2\{0} for every prime p.
claim_reviews.evidence: Transitivity has only been verified computationally for primes p ≤ 17,389. The algebraic argument intended to cover all primes is currently incomplete (see other claims). Therefore universality for all p is not yet established.
claim_reviews.concerns: Extend computation further and/or supply a complete non-circular algebraic proof.

claim_reviews.claim: Using Dickson’s classification, the subgroup H=⟨h1,h2⟩ equals SL2(F_p) for every prime p.
claim_reviews.evidence: Step (3) uses the presumed transitivity |orbit|=p^2−1 to lower-bound |H|, which is exactly what the argument is trying to prove, creating a circular dependency. The Cartan-normalizer exclusion relies on an incorrect statement (common eigenvector ⇒ common eigenvalue). No proof that H lacks a common invariant 1-dimensional subspace is provided, so containment in a Borel or Cartan subgroup is not ruled out rigorously.
claim_reviews.concerns: Provide a non-circular size estimate for H or an independent argument excluding all maximal subgroups.

claim_reviews.claim: h1 is not contained in any Borel subgroup because its (2,1) entry is 1 (≠0).
claim_reviews.evidence: A matrix may lie in a Borel subgroup after conjugation even if it is not upper-triangular in the standard basis. What must be shown is that H fixes no 1-dimensional subspace of F_p^2; the given argument does not address this.
claim_reviews.concerns: Need an invariant-subspace analysis rather than checking a single matrix in one basis.

claim_reviews.claim: h1 and h2 never share an eigenvector modulo any prime p.
claim_reviews.evidence: The supplied proof assumes a shared eigenvector forces a shared eigenvalue, which is false: a vector can be an eigenvector for two matrices with different eigenvalues. No calculation of eigenvectors is provided, so the statement is unsubstantiated.
claim_reviews.concerns: Compute eigenvectors of h1 and h2 modulo p and show they cannot coincide, or supply another rigorous argument.

claim_reviews.claim: Exhaustive BFS confirms transitivity for all 1,229 primes ≤10,000 in 133 s on 112 CPU cores (OpenMP).
claim_reviews.evidence: The claimed workload (~Σ p^2 ≈ 1.1×10^11 vector updates) divided over 112 cores in 133 s implies ~7×10^7 updates per second, well within plausible CPU throughput. Open-source code is provided, so result is reproducible.

overall_assessment: The computational portion is solid and reproduces known behaviour up to p=17,389, but the algebraic proof that would extend the result to all primes contains logical gaps and circular reasoning. Several subgroup-exclusion steps use insufficient or incorrect arguments, so the main universality claim is not yet justified. With a corrected algebraic argument or larger-scale computation the paper could be suitable for acceptance.
recommendations: Remove circular dependence on transitivity when estimating |H|.
Provide a rigorous test that H has no invariant 1-dimensional subspace (rules out Borel and split Cartan cases).
Correct the eigenvector argument for exclusion from nonsplit Cartan normalizers.
Either furnish a non-circular size lower bound or use known generation criteria (e.g., Lubotzky’s or Breuillard–Green–Tao) to show H=SL2(F_p).
Extend computations beyond 17,389 or supply probabilistic heuristics to cover remaining primes.
certification_justification: The computational observations are novel and plausible but, without a complete proof and with limited literature cross-validation, the result does not yet meet silver or gold standards.
